perturbation.original_text: This Agreement, including all matters of construction, validity and performance, shall be governed by, construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of Washington, as applied to contracts made and to be fully performed in such state, without regard to its conflict of law rules.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement, including all matters of construction, validity and performance, shall be governed by, construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of Washington, as applied to contracts made and to be fully performed in such state, with regard to its conflict of law rules.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'without regard' to 'with regard' to its conflict of law rules. This can lead to the application of another state's laws which may be less favorable to consumers, depending on the specific issue in dispute. The original text waived the conflict of law rules of Washington, ensuring that Washington law will always apply.
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 62A.1-301 dictates when and how Washington law applies to transactions with connections to other jurisdictions. By changing 'without regard to' to 'with regard to', the modified text now allows for the application of another jurisdiction's laws if Washington's conflict of law rules dictate so. If this change introduces another jurisdiction's consumer protection laws that are weaker, the contract violates the spirit (and possibly the letter) of Washington's consumer protection framework.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 62A.1-301: Territorial applicability; parties' power to choose applicable law. Print RCWs > Title 62A > Chapter 62A.1 > Section 62A.1-301 62A.1-206 << 62A.1-301 >> 62A.1-302 PDF RCW 62A.1-301 Territorial applicability; parties' power to choose applicable law. (a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this state and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. (b) In the absence of an agreement effective under subsection (a) of this section, and except as provided in subsection (c) of this section, this title applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state. (c) If one of the following provisions of this title specifies the applicable law, that provision governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the law so specified: (1) RCW 62A.2-402 ; (2) RCW 62A.2A-105 and 62A.2A-106 ; (3) RCW 62A.4-102 ; (4) RCW 62A.4A-507 ; (5) RCW 62A.5-116 ; (6) RCW 62A.8-110 ; (7) RCW 62A.9A-301 through 62A.9A-307 ; and (8) RCW 62A.12-107 . [ 2023 c 266 s 103 ; 2012 c 214 s 115 .] NOTES: Construction — Effective date — 2023 c 266: See notes following RCW 62A.12-101 . Application — Savings — 2012 c 214: See notes following RCW 62A.1-101 .
perturbation.justification: The change from 'without regard' to 'with regard' concerning conflict of law rules in the contract directly contradicts the original intent of ensuring Washington law always applies. RCW 62A.1-301(a) states that parties may agree that the law either of this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. By including 'with regard' to conflict of law rules, the changed text allows for the application of another jurisdiction's laws, potentially weakening consumer protection under Washington law, depending on the conflict of law analysis, therefore creating a legally significant contradiction as it removes the certainty of Washington law application.

perturbation.original_text: A prevailing Party shall be entitled to an award of its reasonable attorneys’ fees and costs arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: A prevailing Party may be entitled to an award of its attorneys’ fees and costs arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall' to 'may,' the entitlement to attorney's fees becomes discretionary rather than mandatory. This introduces uncertainty and discourages parties, especially those with limited resources, from pursuing legitimate claims, which can violate principles of fair access to justice and effective remedy.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Washington Consumer Protection Act (RCW 19.86.090) allows for the recovery of attorneys’ fees for successful plaintiffs in certain consumer protection cases. Although this NDA may not directly fall under CPA, weakening the right to attorney's fees generally disincentivizes consumers from enforcing their rights in disputes related to the contract, indirectly undermining the goals of consumer protection laws.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.86.090: Civil action for damages—Treble damages authorized—Action by governmental entities. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.86 > Section 19.86.090 19.86.085 << 19.86.090 >> 19.86.093 PDF RCW 19.86.090 Civil action for damages — Treble damages authorized — Action by governmental entities. Any person who is injured in his or her business or property by a violation of RCW 19.86.020 , 19.86.030 , 19.86.040 , 19.86.050 , or 19.86.060 , or any person so injured because he or she refuses to accede to a proposal for an arrangement which, if consummated, would be in violation of RCW 19.86.030 , 19.86.040 , 19.86.050 , or 19.86.060 , may bring a civil action in superior court to enjoin further violations, to recover the actual damages sustained by him or her, or both, together with the costs of the suit, including a reasonable attorney's fee. In addition, the court may, in its discretion, increase the award of damages up to an amount not to exceed three times the actual damages sustained: PROVIDED, That such increased damage award for violation of RCW 19.86.020 may not exceed twenty-five thousand dollars: PROVIDED FURTHER, That such person may bring a civil action in the district court to recover his or her actual damages, except for damages which exceed the amount specified in RCW 3.66.020 , and the costs of the suit, including reasonable attorney's fees. The district court may, in its discretion, increase the award of damages to an amount not more than three times the actual damages sustained, but such increased damage award shall not exceed twenty-five thousand dollars. For the purpose of this section, "person" includes the counties, municipalities, and all political subdivisions of this state. Whenever the state of Washington is injured, directly or indirectly, by reason of a violation of RCW 19.86.030 , 19.86.040 , 19.86.050 , or 19.86.060 , it may sue therefor in superior court to recover the actual damages sustained by it, whether direct or indirect, and to recover th
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates attorney's fees for the prevailing party ('shall be entitled'), while the modified version makes it discretionary ('may be entitled'). RCW 19.86.090, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that a person injured by a violation may bring a civil action and recover 'the costs of the suit, including a reasonable attorney's fee.' By changing 'shall' to 'may,' the contract weakens the entitlement to attorney's fees, which could discourage parties from pursuing legitimate claims, indirectly undermining the goals of consumer protection laws and the ability to recover attorney's fees as outlined in RCW 19.86.090.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall take reasonable security precautions to protect the Confidential Information, at least as strict as the precautions it takes to protect its own confidential and proprietary information of a similar nature.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party shall take security precautions to protect the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'reasonable' qualifier and the comparison to internal security standards significantly weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. This vague standard creates a loophole that could fall below industry security standards.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this NDA deals with business information, if the 'Confidential Information' exchanged included personal information of Washington residents, the weakened security obligations could violate RCW 19.255, which requires reasonable security measures to protect personal information. Removing the 'reasonable' qualifier and the comparison to internal security standards creates a loophole that could fall below industry security standards.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Chapter 19.255 RCW: PERSONAL INFORMATION—NOTICE OF SECURITY BREACHES Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.255 Complete Chapter HTML PDF Chapter 19.255 RCW PERSONAL INFORMATION — NOTICE OF SECURITY BREACHES Sections HTML PDF 19.255.005 Definitions. HTML PDF 19.255.010 Personal information — Notice of security breaches. HTML PDF 19.255.020 Liability of processors, businesses, and vendors. HTML PDF 19.255.030 Federal law — Covered entities — Financial institutions. HTML PDF 19.255.040 Consumer protection.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates 'reasonable security precautions' and compares them to internal standards, while the changed text only requires 'security precautions,' omitting the reasonableness standard and the comparison. The Perturbation Explanation details that removing 'reasonable' weakens the obligation and could fall below industry standards. The Law Explanation specifies RCW 19.255, which requires reasonable security measures to protect personal information, especially for Washington residents. Scraped Law Snippet 1 provides the chapter heading for RCW 19.255, 'PERSONAL INFORMATION—NOTICE OF SECURITY BREACHES,' confirming that this law is about personal information security. This confirms the change removes the legal obligation to have reasonable security measures, contradicting RCW 19.255, thus falling under the definition of Omission Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Upon written request by the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall return to the Disclosing Party, or shall destroy in a manner satisfactory to the Disclosing Party, all tangible or electronic forms of Confidential Information, including any and all copies of the Confidential Information or notes containing the Confidential Information, and shall provide a written certification to the Disclosing Party that all tangible forms of the Confidential Information have been returned or destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon written request by the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall return to the Disclosing Party all tangible or electronic forms of Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirements to destroy the information if requested, ensure a satisfactory manner of destruction, and to provide a written certification that the information has been returned or destroyed weakens the disclosing party's ability to control the destiny of sensitive information.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the requirements around destruction and certification of destruction, the revised clause fails to adequately protect trade secrets under the Washington UTSA (RCW 19.108). A key aspect of trade secret protection is maintaining its secrecy, and the absence of destruction and certification requirements creates a loophole that allows the recipient to retain the information indefinitely, increasing the risk of unauthorized disclosure and violating the UTSA's objective.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Revised Code of Washington § 19.108.010 (2022) - Definitions. :: 2022 Revised Code of Washington :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Rev. Code of Wash. 2023 Rev. Code of Wash. 2022 Rev. Code of Wash. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Revised Code of Washington Title 19 - Business Regulations—Miscellaneous Chapter 19.108 - Uniform Trade Secrets Act. 19.108.010 - Definitions. Universal Citation: WA Rev Code § 19.108.010 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Next RCW 19.108.010 Definitions. Unless the context clearly requires otherwise, the definitions set forth in this section apply throughout this chapter. (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means; (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, tru
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text omits requirements related to the destruction and certification of confidential information, weakening trade secret protection. The Law Explanation states that the absence of destruction and certification requirements increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure, violating the objective of the Washington UTSA (RCW 19.108). Scraped Law Snippet 1 references Chapter 19.108 RCW: UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT, indicating the legal framework for trade secret protection. Scraped Law Snippet 2, RCW 19.108.010, defines 'Improper means' which includes breach of a duty to maintain secrecy. By removing the recipient's obligation to destroy or certify destruction of confidential information upon request, the revised clause increases the risk of a breach, contradicting the UTSA's aim to protect trade secrets and maintain their secrecy.
